plan.title: Rough-variance scaling identification theorem
plan.refinement_query: Refine the rough-variance scaling theorem for SPY US Equity. Replace generic conjectural phrasing with a sharper theorem candidate. Tighten the assumptions on the Volterra kernel singularity and roughness exponent. State the observable realized-variance proxy precisely, connect it to the Bloomberg feature panel, and list explicit empirical signatures and failure conditions. Make the theorem specific to SPY US Equity, not a generic placeholder.
plan.rationale: Strongest current theorem family; needs tighter assumptions, clearer observables, and explicit failure conditions.

result.response:
Research artifact: Rough-variance scaling identification theorem
Status: research_conjecture
Score: 0.696

Statement:
Conjecture: Assume the latent log-volatility process X admits a Volterra representation X_t = X_0 + ∫_0^t K(t,s) dW_s with local kernel singularity K(t,s) ~ c (t-s)^{H-1/2} for H in (0,1/2). Then, as Δ ↓ 0, the increment variance satisfies E[(X_{t+Δ}-X_t)^2] = C_X Δ^{2H} + o(Δ^{2H}), and any realized-variance proxy constructed from sufficiently fine observations for SPY US Equity inherits the same scaling exponent after lower-order noise correction: E[RV_Δ] = a_0 + a_1 Δ^{2H} + o(Δ^{2H}).

Assumptions:
- H > 0 and H < 1/2.
- The volatility driver admits a Volterra representation with local singularity exponent H-1/2.
- The observed realized-variance proxy is asymptotically consistent up to lower-order noise distortion.
- Empirical scope anchored to SPY US Equity.

Let us further agree that K(t, s) = 0 for s ≤t; such kernels are said to be of Volterra type. Note that eW is a standard Brownian motion, correlated with W (with correlation ϱ), whereas bW is a Riemann–Liouville fractional Brownian motion (fBm) with Hurst index H. Prototypical rough volatility dynamics are given by  dS t  √ −1  dXt =  S t = vtd eWt, or, equivalently, 2Vtdt + Vt deWt, (8.5)  vt = σ bWt, t ,  Vt = σ2 bWt, t , with the rough stochastic volatility (resp., variance) process v (resp., V) given as an explicit function of the fBm via some continuous “volatility function” σ = σ(x, t)

. This model, introduced in [129], takes the form38 dS t √ = VtdBt, S t u (4.4) 1 1 Z n Vu = Vt + λ θt(s) −Vs ds + ν pVsdWs o, 1 2 −H Γ(H t (u −s) + 12) for u ≥t, where H ∈(0, 1/2) is the Hurst exponent, Γ is the Gamma function, and the parameters λ, ν, ϱ have the same meaning as in the Heston model. The mean reversion level parameter θt(·) is now an Ft-measurable function which makes the model time consistent; see [131]. Setting the Hurst parameter to 1/2, we retrieve the classical Heston model (4.1). We recognize in (4.4) the power-law kernel of the Mandelbrot–Van Ness representation of the fractional Brownian motion (fBm); hence the name “rough Heston.” This model enjoys the nice statistical properties of rough volatility models. In addition, as will be seen in Section 4.2.3, it provides remarkable fits of the volatility surface while remaining almost as tractable as the classical Heston model, as explained in Section 4.2

Chapter 2 Pricing under rough volatility24 Christian Bayer, Peter K. Friz, Jim Gatheral 2.1 Introduction From an analysis of the time series of realized variance using recent high frequency data, Gatheral, Jaisson, and Rosenbaum [166] showed that the logarithm of realized variance behaves essentially as a fractional Brownian motion (fBm) with Hurst exponent H of order 0.1, at any reasonable time scale. The following stationary rough fractional stochastic volatility (RFSV) model was proposed: dS t = µtdt + VtdZt, S t Vt = exp{Xt}, t ∈[0, T], (2.1) where µt is a suitable drift term, Zt is a standard Brownian motion, Xt is a fractional Ornstein– Uhlenbeck process (fOU process for short) satisfying dXt = νdWHt −α(Xt −m)dt, where m ∈R, and ν and α are positive parameters (see [92]), with Z and WH correlated in general

(10.30) (bWt −bWs)lWm−l l l=0 We are now in position to define a model (Π, Γ) that gives rigorous meaning to the interpre- tation we gave above for Ξ, I(Ξ), ΞI(Ξ), . . .

. Although the rate function here is not given in a very useful form, it is possible to expand it in small y and thus compute (explicitly in terms of the model parameters) higher-order moderate deviations. In [40] this was related to implied volatility skew expansions. 10.4 Rough Volterra dynamics for volatility 10.4.1 Motivation from market microstructure Rosenbaum and coworkers, [126, 129, 131] show that stylized facts of modern market micro- structure naturally give rise to fractional dynamics and leverage effects. Specifically, they con- struct a sequence of Hawkes processes, suitably rescaled in time and space, that converges in law to a rough volatility model of rough Heston form √ dS t/S t = VtdBt ≡√v ϱdWt + ¯ϱdWt , (10.52) Z t a −bVs Z t c √Vs Vt = V0 + 1 ds + 1 dWs. 0 (t −s) 2 −H 0 (t −s) 2 −H (As earlier, W, W are independent Brownian motions.) Similar to the case of the classical Heston model, the square-root provides both pain (with regard to any methods that rely on sufficient

. The rough volatility model can be seen as an extension of the MRW with an extra parameter allowing one to tune at will the roughness of volatility. More precisely, it postulates that the log- volatility is a fractional Brownian motion with Hurst exponent H less than 1/2 (i.e., “rougher” that the Brownian motion) while the MRW model formally corresponds to H →0. Indeed, empirical data reveal that volatility is slightly less rough than what the MRW posits, at least for stock indices. Calibration suggests H ≈0.1 for the S&P500, whereas very recent (2022) work by Wu, Bacry, and Muzy suggests that single stock returns are better adjusted with H = 0. The next episode of this long saga came in 2009 when Zumbach noticed a subtle, yet crucial, aspect of empirical financial time series: they are not statistically invariant upon time reversal. Past and future are not equivalent, whereas most models up to that date, including the MRW, did not distinguish past from future

218 Chapter 10. Regularity structure for rough volatility Comparing the expressions in Lemmas 10.13 and 10.12, we see that morally we have to subtract Z t s )m−1 dt m φ(t) K ε(t, t)(bWε −bWε from the model, which will give us a new model ˆΠε. Of course we have to be careful that this step preserves “Chen’s relation” ˆΠεsΓst = ˆΠεt ; see Theorem 10.15. If we interpret K ε as an approximation to the Volterra kernel, we see that the expression C ε(t) := K ε(t, t), t ≥0, will correspond to something like “0H−12 = ∞” in the limit ε ↓0. We indeed have the following upper bound. Lemma 10.14. For all s, t ∈R we have |K ε(s, t)| ≲εH−12 . 2 ≲εH−12 .Proof. |K ε(s, t)| ≲ε−2 R B(t,cε) dx R B(x,cε) du |s −u|H−1 Our hope is now that the new model ˆΠε converges to Π in a suitable sense

8.5. Rough volatility at criticality: H = 0 179 8.5.1 Skew flattening in the rough Bergomi model as H ↓0 Unfortunately, this is not the case with the standard rough stochastic volatility models of rough Bergomi type. Indeed, [133] shows that the ATM skew converges to zero as H tends to zero. More precisely, the authors of that paper show that the implied volatility smile becomes symmet- ric as H ↓0, and, using the usual normalization of the kernel, the rough Bergomi model actually weakly converges to the Black–Scholes model as H ↓0; see [133, Section 2.2]. Superficially, this means that the constant cH ↓0 “wins” over the steepening of the power law, but, in fact, the flattening of the skew is a consequence of a decorrelation phenomenon: When H ↓0, the martingale part R 0t √VsdWs of the log-price X weakly converges to a time-change Bξ([0,t]) of an independent Brownian motion B [133, Theorem 2.1], where ξ denotes a Gaussian multiplicative chaos [259]

40 Chapter 2. Pricing under rough volatility where bW is a Volterra process30 with the scaling property V[ bWu] = u2H. So far, bW behaves just like fBm. However, the dependence structure is different. Specifically, for v > u, Eh i = u2H G v bWv bWu u where, for x ≥1 and with γ = 12 −H, Z 1 ds 1 −2γ G(x) = 2H = xγ2F1(1, γ, 2 −γ, x), (2.16) 0 (1 −s)γ (x −s)γ 1 −γ where 2F1(·) denotes the hypergeometric function. Remark 2.4.1. The dependence structure of the Volterra process bW is markedly different from that of fBm, with the Molchan–Golosov kernel K(u, s) given by 1 1 −H, H −1 H + 1 s −u , 0 < s < t, K(u, s) = cH 2F1 (u −s)γ 2 2, 2, s for some constant cH. In particular, for small H, correlations drop precipitously as the ratio u/v moves away from 1. We also need covariances of the Brownian motion Z with the Volterra process bW. With v ≥u, these are given by 2 −(v −u)H+ 12 o Eh i = ϱDH nvH+ 1 bWvZu and 1 i 2 Eh = ϱ DHuH+ Zv bWu where, for future convenience, we have defined the constant √ 2H DH = 1 . H + 2 These two formulae may be conveniently combined as 2 1 −(v −min(u, v))H+ 12 o

10.2. The rough pricing regularity structure 211 Remark 10.2.1. It is useful here and in the sequel to consider as a sanity check the special case H = 12 in which we recover the “level-2” rough path structure as introduced in [147, Chapter 13]. More specifically, if we take a Hölder exponent α := 21 −κ < 12, we may choose M = 1. Then condition (10.25) is precisely the familiar condition α > 1/3. The interpretation for the symbols in S is as follows: Ξ should be understood as an abstract representation of the white noise ξ belonging to the Brownian motion W, i.e., ξ = ˙W where the derivative is taken in the distributional sense. Note that since we set Wx = 0 for x ≤0, we have ˙W(φ) = 0 for φ ∈C∞c ((−∞, 0)). The symbol I(. . .) has the intuitive meaning of “integration against the Volterra kernel,” so that I(Ξ) represents the integration of the white noise against the Volterra kernel, i.e., √ Z t 2H |t −r|H−12 dWr, 0 which is nothing but the fBm bWt. Symbols like ΞI(Ξ)m = Ξ · I(Ξ) · . . . · I(Ξ) or I(Ξ)m = I(Ξ) · . . . · I(Ξ) should be read as products between the objects above

QuantAI currently has a working Bloomberg historical warehouse and a working empirical feature layer. BQL is environment-dependent and currently unavailable unless the local environment includes Bloomberg's BQL object model. Options-surface extraction is partially wired but still requires stronger contract selection and normalization when the requested put/call subset is not represented in the sampled chain.

## Next research actions
1. Convert these memory notes into retrieval-ready empirical evidence.
2. Route market-state questions to this Bloomberg memory before invoking theorem synthesis.
3. Strengthen options contract selection so put/call filters do not collapse to zero usable rows.
4. Build calibration-specific memory notes once surface extraction is stable.

fusion_hits.title: Rough Volatility.pdf | page 137 | chunk 2
fusion_hits.excerpt: We discuss the role of the forward variance curve in establishing (perfect) hedging in rough models and present a hands-on empirical study illustrating the role of the Hurst parameter (driving the roughness of the paths) in the hedging performance for hedging VIX options.
fusion_hits.context_text: . To calibrate VIX option smiles via rough volatility, we consider extended lognormal models by adding volatility modulation through an independent stochastic factor in the Volterra integral which preserves part of the analytical tractability of the lognormal setting by extending it through an affine structure, which makes it possible to develop approximate option pricing and calibration algorithms based on Fourier transform techniques. In this chapter, we showcase these ideas in relatively transparent and illustrative settings. We discuss the role of the forward variance curve in establishing (perfect) hedging in rough models and present a hands-on empirical study illustrating the role of the Hurst parameter (driving the roughness of the paths) in the hedging performance for hedging VIX options. 6.2 A theoretical framework The purpose of this section is to illustrate an infinite-dimensional Markov nature of rough volatil- ity models, which enables us to hedge options without any “memory” of the past

metadata.text: . To calibrate VIX option smiles via rough volatility, we consider extended lognormal models by adding volatility modulation through an independent stochastic factor in the Volterra integral which preserves part of the analytical tractability of the lognormal setting by extending it through an affine structure, which makes it possible to develop approximate option pricing and calibration algorithms based on Fourier transform techniques. In this chapter, we showcase these ideas in relatively transparent and illustrative settings. We discuss the role of the forward variance curve in establishing (perfect) hedging in rough models and present a hands-on empirical study illustrating the role of the Hurst parameter (driving the roughness of the paths) in the hedging performance for hedging VIX options. 6.2 A theoretical framework The purpose of this section is to illustrate an infinite-dimensional Markov nature of rough volatil- ity models, which enables us to hedge options without any “memory” of the past

theorem_result.query: Refine the rough-variance scaling theorem for SPY US Equity. Replace generic conjectural phrasing with a sharper theorem candidate. Tighten the assumptions on the Volterra kernel singularity and roughness exponent. State the observable realized-variance proxy precisely, connect it to the Bloomberg feature panel, and list explicit empirical signatures and failure conditions. Make the theorem specific to SPY US Equity, not a generic placeholder.

selected.title: Rough-variance scaling identification theorem
selected.statement: Conjecture: Assume the latent log-volatility process X admits a Volterra representation X_t = X_0 + ∫_0^t K(t,s) dW_s with local kernel singularity K(t,s) ~ c (t-s)^{H-1/2} for H in (0,1/2). Then, as Δ ↓ 0, the increment variance satisfies E[(X_{t+Δ}-X_t)^2] = C_X Δ^{2H} + o(Δ^{2H}), and any realized-variance proxy constructed from sufficiently fine observations for SPY US Equity inherits the same scaling exponent after lower-order noise correction: E[RV_Δ] = a_0 + a_1 Δ^{2H} + o(Δ^{2H}).
assumptions: The volatility driver admits a Volterra representation with local singularity exponent H-1/2.
The observed realized-variance proxy is asymptotically consistent up to lower-order noise distortion.
Empirical scope anchored to SPY US Equity.
selected.motivation: Connect latent rough-volatility regularity to an empirically estimable variance-scaling slope.
failure_conditions: Estimated scaling slope is unstable across windows or frequencies.
Jump-share dominates the short-horizon variance proxy.
No persistent roughness signal appears in the Bloomberg feature panel.
No exact excerpt currently pins down every constant or normalization.
Add explicit symbolic tasks with lhs/rhs identities or qualitative constraints.

evidence.excerpt: 158 Chapter 8. Asymptotics p 1 −ϱ2 and Z t eWt := ϱ Wt + ϱWt and bWt := (K ∗˙W)(t) = K(t, s)dWs, (8.3) 0 with (prototypical) kernel K = KH, with H ∈(0, 12], given by √ KH(t, s) = 2H(t −s)H−12 for t > 0, (8.4) with prefactor chosen such that bW1 has unit variance. Let us further agree that K(t, s) = 0 for s ≤t; such kernels are said to be of Volterra type. Note that eW is a standard Brownian motion, correlated with W (with correlation ϱ), whereas bW is a Riemann–Liouville fractional Brownian motion (fBm) with Hurst index H. Prototypical rough volatility dynamics are given by  dS t  √ −1  dXt =  S t = vtd eWt, or, equivalently, 2Vtdt + Vt deWt, (8.5)  vt = σ bWt, t ,  Vt = σ2 bWt, t , with the rough stochastic volatility (resp., variance) process v (resp., V) given as an explicit function of the fBm via some continuous “volatility function” σ = σ(x, t)

. This model, introduced in [129], takes the form38 dS t √ = VtdBt, S t u (4.4) 1 1 Z n Vu = Vt + λ θt(s) −Vs ds + ν pVsdWs o, 1 2 −H Γ(H t (u −s) + 12) for u ≥t, where H ∈(0, 1/2) is the Hurst exponent, Γ is the Gamma function, and the parameters λ, ν, ϱ have the same meaning as in the Heston model. The mean reversion level parameter θt(·) is now an Ft-measurable function which makes the model time consistent; see [131]. Setting the Hurst parameter to 1/2, we retrieve the classical Heston model (4.1). We recognize in (4.4) the power-law kernel of the Mandelbrot–Van Ness representation of the fractional Brownian motion (fBm); hence the name “rough Heston.” This model enjoys the nice statistical properties of rough volatility models. In addition, as will be seen in Section 4.2.3, it provides remarkable fits of the volatility surface while remaining almost as tractable as the classical Heston model, as explained in Section 4.2

Chapter 2 Pricing under rough volatility24 Christian Bayer, Peter K. Friz, Jim Gatheral 2.1 Introduction From an analysis of the time series of realized variance using recent high frequency data, Gatheral, Jaisson, and Rosenbaum [166] showed that the logarithm of realized variance behaves essentially as a fractional Brownian motion (fBm) with Hurst exponent H of order 0.1, at any reasonable time scale. The following stationary rough fractional stochastic volatility (RFSV) model was proposed: dS t = µtdt + VtdZt, S t Vt = exp{Xt}, t ∈[0, T], (2.1) where µt is a suitable drift term, Zt is a standard Brownian motion, Xt is a fractional Ornstein– Uhlenbeck process (fOU process for short) satisfying dXt = νdWHt −α(Xt −m)dt, where m ∈R, and ν and α are positive parameters (see [92]), with Z and WH correlated in general

# Bloomberg Research Memory: SPY US Equity

## As of
- Date: 2026-03-26

## Market state
- Last price: 645.09
- Last daily return: -0.0180202
- Current drawdown: -0.0724669
- Volatility regime: compressed_volatility
- Roughness signature: rough_or_antipersistent
- Mean-reversion signature: weak_mean_reversion

## Realized volatility panel
- Realized vol 5: 0.0277618
- Realized vol 21: 0.0426373
- Realized vol 63: 0.0627613

## Roughness / Hurst-style panel
- Hurst variance scaling 5: NA
- Hurst variance scaling 21: 0
- Hurst variance scaling 63: 0.0180497

## OU / mean-reversion panel
- OU speed 5: NA
- OU speed 21: 0.0823583
- OU speed 63: 0.0219479

## Jump / discontinuity panel
- Jump share 5: 0.234451
- Jump share 21: 0.114189
- Jump share 63: 0.0863611

## Volatility clustering panel
- Abs-return ACF 5: -0.0685379
- Abs-return ACF 21: -0.291613
- Abs-return ACF 63: 0.0329935

## Return autocorrelation panel
- Return autocorr 5: -0.746464
- Return autocorr 21: -0.366119
- Return autocorr 63: -0.209893

## Volume anomaly panel
- Volume z-score 5: -0.701767
- Volume z-score 21: -0.0243653
- Volume z-score 63: 0.510649

## Historical coverage
- Rows: 41730
- Start: 1993-01-29
- End: 2026-03-26

## Research interpretation
This note summarizes the local Bloomberg-derived empirical state for SPY US Equity. It is intended to support theorem generation, empirical falsification, calibration design, and hypothesis ranking inside QuantAI.

Primary current interpretations:
- Volatility regime classification: **compressed_volatility**
- Roughness classification: **rough_or_antipersistent**
- Mean-reversion classification: **weak_mean_reversion**

## Research prompts QuantAI can pursue
1. Test whether roughness estimates co-move with realized-volatility regimes.
2. Test whether jump share rises in high-volatility drawdown states.
3. Compare OU-style speeds across windows for stability or regime breaks.
4. Examine whether absolute-return persistence supports rough-vol style memory.

## Snapshot linkage
- Feature table present: yes
- Options snapshot attempted: yes
